channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite - SpaceNews
item.description:
1. FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite  SpaceNews
  2. F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry  The New York Times
  3. The U.S. just approved a giant space mirror to test ‘sunlight on demand.’ Low Earth orbit is getting weird  The Conversation
  4. FCC Approves Reflect Orbital's Space Mirror Satellite That Astronomers Hate  PCMag
  5. FCC Approves Extremely Controversial Space Mirror Satellite That Could "Flash Blind" Drivers And Ruin Astronomy  IFLScience

item.title: Darwin's 150‑year‑old hillside steps mystery may have a new answer from virtual grazing animals - Phys.org
item.description: Darwin's 150‑year‑old hillside steps mystery may have a new answer from virtual grazing animals  Phys.org

item.title: Saturn's rings are so young that dinosaurs would have looked up at a ringless planet, and measurements from the Cassini probe suggest they are being pulled into the planet fast enough to disappear entirely - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Saturn's rings are so young that dinosaurs would have looked up at a ringless planet, and measurements from the Cassini probe suggest they are being pulled into the planet fast enough to disappear entirely  Space Daily
  2. Saturn’s rings may be far younger than the planet itself — Cassini-era estimates have placed their age anywhere from about 10 million to a few hundred million years, meaning the rings we see today may not have existed for most of the age of dinosaurs.  Space Daily

  4. Earthquakes May Never be the Same After Latest Discovery  Yardbarker
  5. AI has detected hidden slow movements beneath California’s San Andreas Fault earlier missed by scientists: Can it cause a massive earthquake?  The Times of India

item.title: We tend to assume life needs a star to begin, but a 2025 study found that some moons carried into deep space by planets expelled during supernova explosions could preserve subsurface oceans for billions of years — heated not by sunlight, but by the repeat - Space Daily
item.description:
1. We tend to assume life needs a star to begin, but a 2025 study found that some moons carried into deep space by planets expelled during supernova explosions could preserve subsurface oceans for billions of years — heated not by sunlight, but by the repeat  Space Daily
  2. Astronomers believe the Milky Way may contain billions, perhaps even trillions, of rogue planets drifting without close stars — and a 2026 study found one 9,800 light-years away that was likely born in a planetary system before being thrown into the dark  Space Daily

item.title: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock - Space Daily
item.description: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists Say They’ve Identified an Earth-Like Planet Right Next Door - Futurism
item.description:
1. Scientists Say They’ve Identified an Earth-Like Planet Right Next Door  Futurism
  2. Alien life on nearby exoplanet more likely than previously thought  The Jerusalem Post
  3. Scientists say 'super-Earth' located 25 light-years away from us may be a top place to look for life  Yahoo
  4. In November 2025, UC Irvine astronomers announced a rocky super-Earth called GJ 251 c orbiting in a habitable zone less than 20 light-years away — close enough that the next generation of ground-based telescopes may actually photograph it directly  Space Daily
  5. This Nearby Exoplanet Is Likely Nice And Rocky. But It Also Sits On The Dangerous "Cosmic Shoreline"  IFLScience

item.title: How the Rule-Breaking Octopus Is Rewriting the Evolution of Intelligence - Nautilus | Science
item.description:
1. How the Rule-Breaking Octopus Is Rewriting the Evolution of Intelligence  Nautilus | Science
  2. Octopus Brains Defy a Long-Held Rule About Why Animals Evolve Intelligence  ScienceAlert
  3. Scientists Discover Unexpected Intelligence in Solitary Octopus During Jar-Opening Challenge  CPG Click Oil and Gas
  4. Ecological factors, not social behavior, explain brain size in cephalopods  Phys.org

item.title: NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently - Space Daily
item.description:
1. NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently  Space Daily
  2. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  3. Voyager Spacecraft: The Ultimate Power Management Challenge?  EE Times
  4. 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel  Yahoo
  5. On November 18, 2026, Voyager 1 becomes the first thing humanity has ever built to sit a full light-day away — a full day for any signal to reach it.  19FortyFive

item.description: England field mouse survives cat attack, gets treatment, and bolts back into the wild  Yahoo

item.title: We assumed water had to be delivered to planets by comets or asteroids — but a 2025 Nature study found that sub-Neptunes, the most common type of planet in the galaxy, can forge their own oceans by reacting hydrogen atmospheres with molten rock deep - Space Daily

item.title: An exoplanet 124 light-years away may harbour a global ocean beneath a hydrogen-rich atmosphere. Webb has produced two tentative hints of dimethyl sulfide—a gas produced mainly by marine microorganisms on Earth—but independent analyses dispute - Space Daily
item.description:
